text:
Section D - Key Findings Summary


======================================================================
 KEY FINDINGS — AD688 Final EDA
======================================================================

DATASET OVERVIEW
----------------------------------
- Raw: 72,498 rows × 131 columns  |  Cleaned: 67,572 rows × 117 columns
- Date range: 2024-05-01 → 2024-09-30  (5 months)
- Scope: data / analytics / tech occupations (Lightcast pre-filter)


TOPIC 2.1 — AI vs. Non-AI Careers
----------------------------------
1. Classification breakdown (3-tier combined classifier):
   - Traditional Analytics / BI        57,870  (85.6%)
   - Advanced Analytics / Engineering   9,398  (13.9%)
   - AI/ML Core                           304   (0.4%)
   → Only 1.8% of postings explicitly list specific ML framework skills
     (TensorFlow, PyTorch, Keras, etc.).
   → All 304 AI/ML Core jobs were flagged by STRICT TITLE KEYWORDS —
     the Lightcast LOT taxonomy flagged ZERO as AI/ML Core,
     confirming the taxonomy is too broad to distinguish true AI roles.

2. Salary gradient (confirmed):
   Advanced Analytics / Engineering   Median ,000  (P25 k  P75 k)
   AI/ML Core                         Median ,000  (P25 $ 97k  P75 k)
   Traditional Analytics / BI         Median ,000  (P25 $ 75k  P75 k)
   → Counter-intuitive: Advanced Analytics (architects, senior data engineers)
     out-earns labeled 'AI/ML Core' roles — likely due to seniority mix.
     The salary premium for AI roles exists but is less extreme than expected.

3. Remote work — SURPRISING finding:
   Fully Remote:  AI/ML Core 6.6%  |  Advanced 25.0%  |  Traditional 16.4%
   On-Site:       AI/ML Core 13.2% |  Advanced  1.1%  |  Traditional  1.6%
   → AI/ML Core roles are the LEAST remote and MOST on-site of the three tiers.
     Advanced Analytics/Engineering is the most remote-friendly tier (25%).
     ~78% of all postings do not specify remote type.


SECTION B — AI/ML CORE DEEP DIVE
---------------------------------
4. Monthly growth trend (AI/ML Core):
   May 51 → Jun 60 → Jul 60 → Aug 65 → Sep 68  (+33% over 5 months)
   Market share: 0.37% (May) → 0.47% (Sep)
   → Despite tiny absolute numbers, AI/ML Core postings show a consistent
     upward trend throughout the observation window.

5. Geographic distribution (AI/ML Core volume):
   Top states: Texas, California, Virginia, North Carolina, Illinois,
               Maryland, Pennsylvania, New York
   Top by concentration: Maryland, Washington D.C., Connecticut, Alabama,
               Minnesota lead in AI/ML Core share relative to total postings.
   → AI/ML Core is NOT exclusively a coastal/tech-hub phenomenon.

6. Industry distribution (AI/ML Core):
   Highest AI/ML Core CONCENTRATION by industry (min 200 postings):
   Transportation & Warehousing > Mining/Oil & Gas > Information > Finance
   → Surprisingly, Transportation & Warehousing has the highest ML-tool
     requirement rate (1.44%), ahead of Information (0.92%) and Finance (0.90%).
     This likely reflects autonomous vehicle, route optimization, and logistics AI.

7. Top companies hiring AI/ML Core: Honu Services, Capgemini, Penske,
   Deloitte, Disney, Mayo Clinic, Nestlé, Cognizant, US Bank, Travelers
   → Mix of consulting firms, healthcare, automotive, and financial services.

8. Top skills in AI/ML Core: Data Science, Machine Learning, Python,
   Statistics, AI, Mathematics, AWS, Azure, Generative AI, Computer Science
   → Core ML skills + cloud platforms dominate; Generative AI already appears
     in top 25 skills, reflecting 2024 market shift.


TOPIC 2.5 — BA / Data Science / ML Trends
------------------------------------------
9.  Overall posting volume:
    May 13,897 → Jun 13,159 → Jul 11,904 → Aug 14,221 → Sep 14,390
    → Volume dipped in July (summer slowdown) but recovered strongly by Sep.

10. Occupation growth (May → Sep 2024):
    Business / Management Analyst        +51.4%  (fastest growing)
    Data / Data Mining Analyst           +11.0%
    Clinical Analyst                      -3.4%
    Business Intelligence Analyst         -4.5%
    Computer Systems Engineer/Architect  -11.2%
    Market Research Analyst              -15.0%
    → Demand for generalist business analytics roles surged while
      specialized engineering and BI roles declined.

11. Industry growth (May → Sep 2024):
    Information                           +33.2%
    Finance and Insurance                 +15.2%
    Wholesale Trade                       +14.5%
    Health Care and Social Assistance      +7.7%
    Professional/Scientific/Technical     -1.9%
    Administrative & Support Services     -5.8%
    → Information and Finance are the fastest-growing employers of
      data/tech talent; traditionally dominant PSTS sector is flat.

12. AI/ML tool skill penetration (% of postings, by industry):
    Transportation & Warehousing 1.44% | Mining 1.22% | Information 0.92%
    Finance 0.90% | PSTS 0.67% | Education 0.66%
    → Overall penetration is very low (<2%) and FLAT across the 5-month
      window — AI adoption in job postings was NOT accelerating in 2024H2.

13. Salary trend:
    May ,500 → Jun ,000 → Jul ,200 → Aug ,685 → Sep ,000
    → Salary dipped during summer (typical hiring cycle pattern) then
      partially recovered, ending below the May peak. No clear upward trend
      in the short observation window.

title: Machine Learning

Machine Learning

Purpose

This section applies machine learning methods to analyze patterns in the 2024 job market dataset.
We use one unsupervised learning model and two supervised learning models:

KMeans Clustering to identify segments of job postings
Linear Regression to examine the relationship between experience and salary
Logistic Regression to classify whether a job is remote

The purpose of this section is to understand how experience, salary, and work format are connected, and to provide practical implications for job seekers.

Load Libraries and Data

We first import the necessary libraries and load the cleaned dataset.

The dataset contains a large number of job postings and multiple structured variables. This provides a sufficient basis for applying machine learning methods to study salary patterns, experience requirements, and remote work characteristics.

Descriptive Statistics Before building models, we examine the summary statistics of the key numerical variables used in this section.

The summary statistics show substantial variation in both salary and experience requirements across job postings. This suggests that the market includes jobs at very different levels, which supports the use of clustering and predictive models.

KMeans Clustering We apply KMeans clustering using Salary and Minimum Years of Experience to segment job postings into three groups.

The clustering output groups jobs into three segments based on salary and experience. This means that the job market is not uniform. Instead, it contains different layers of opportunities with different levels of pay and required qualifications.









The scatter plot shows three visually distinct salary bands. In general, jobs with lower experience requirements tend to fall into lower salary clusters, while jobs requiring more experience are more likely to appear in higher salary clusters. This suggests a clear segmentation of the labor market into entry-level, mid-level, and higher-paying positions.
For job seekers, this result suggests that career progression is closely tied to experience accumulation. Entry-level roles may offer lower salaries, but they serve as important stepping stones toward better-paying positions.

Linear Regression Next, we use Linear Regression to examine whether minimum years of experience can help explain salary midpoint.



Regression Results
RMSE: 37343.60884288971
R²: 0.25627925864505907
Intercept: 82505.11615984552
Coefficient: 6942.344143590794


The regression results show a positive coefficient, indicating that jobs requiring more experience tend to have higher salary midpoints. However, the R² value is relatively low, which means that experience alone explains only part of the salary variation. Other factors such as job title, location, industry, and technical skills likely also influence salary.









The regression plot shows an overall upward trend, meaning salary midpoint tends to rise as required experience increases. At the same time, the data points are widely scattered around the fitted line, which indicates that salary is influenced by additional variables beyond experience.
This model suggests that experience matters, but experience alone is not enough to guarantee a high salary. Job seekers should not only accumulate experience, but also improve technical skills, target stronger industries, and seek more valuable job titles.

Logistic Regression: Remote Job Classification Finally, we build a Logistic Regression model to classify whether a job is remote based on: Minimum Years of Experience Salary Mid

The model achieves an accuracy of about 46.9%, which indicates relatively weak predictive performance. This suggests that salary midpoint and experience requirement alone are not strong enough to accurately predict whether a job is remote. In other words, remote work is likely influenced by other factors such as company policy, occupation type, industry, and organizational work style.
The confusion matrix shows that the model makes a considerable number of misclassifications for both remote and non-remote jobs. This means the distinction between remote and non-remote positions cannot be captured well using only these two numerical features.
The classification report also shows that the model performs unevenly across classes. This further confirms that remote status depends on more complex factors than salary and experience alone.
For job seekers, this finding is important: remote work opportunities are not simply associated with higher pay or more experience. Instead, candidates looking for remote positions should pay closer attention to: industry and job function company culture and remote policy firms known for flexible work arrangements

Overall Findings Across the three models, several key insights emerge: The job market can be segmented into different clusters based on salary and experience. Experience has a positive relationship with salary, but it explains only part of salary differences. Remote work status is difficult to predict using only salary and experience.
Recommendations for Job Seekers Based on the results above, we recommend that job seekers: Build experience gradually and strategically Develop technical and analytical skills in addition to years of experience Consider industry, occupation, and company policy when targeting remote jobs Focus not only on salary, but also on long-term career progression
Conclusion Overall, the machine learning analysis provides useful insights into how the 2024 job market is structured. KMeans clustering shows clear segmentation across job levels, linear regression confirms that experience is positively associated with salary, and logistic regression suggests that remote work depends on more than simple numerical characteristics. These findings help job seekers better understand how to position themselves in the job market and what factors matter most for salary growth and remote opportunities.

title: AI Careers and Industry Trends:
text:
Artificial intelligence has moved from research labs into mainstream industries, sparking debate over whether it creates or destroys jobs (Briggs and Dong 2023). While AI is automating certain tasks, it is simultaneously generating entirely new occupational categories — from Prompt Engineers to AI Ethicists — that did not exist a decade ago (MIT Technology Review 2024). This section examines how AI-driven career growth compares to job displacement, how traditional roles are being reshaped, and what new occupations are emerging as a direct result of AI advancement.



This analysis uses the Lightcast 2024 job postings dataset (Lightcast 2024) to empirically examine how AI is reshaping the labor market across industries. While much of the existing discourse on AI and employment relies on macroeconomic projections, this study takes a data-driven approach — analyzing real employer demand signals to identify which roles are growing, which are declining, and how job requirements differ between AI-related and traditional occupations. By examining actual job titles, required skills, and posting volumes across sectors, this analysis aims to move beyond speculation and provide concrete evidence of how AI istransforming hiring in 2024.



Goldman Sachs (2023) estimated that generative AI could expose roughly 300 million full-time equivalent jobs globally to automation, while projecting net positive employment outcomes over time — noting that approximately 60% of current U.S. workers hold jobs that did not exist in 1940 (Briggs and Dong 2023). The IMF (2024) found that about 40% of jobs globally face meaningful AI exposure, rising to 60% in high-income economies, stressing that exposure does not always meandisplacement (International Monetary Fund 2024).
The World Economic Forum’s Future of Jobs Report 2025 projected 92 million roles displaced by 2030 alongside 170 million new roles created — a net addition of 78 million jobs (World Economic Forum 2025). The ITIF (2025) found that in 2024 alone, AI-related job creation substantially outpaced displacement, with data center expansion generating over 110,000 construction jobs (Information Technology and Innovation Foundation 2025). Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis researchers (2025) detected early correlations between AI exposure and rising unemployment in certain occupational categories (Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis 2025).
New job titles are proliferating rapidly. The Prompt Engineer role emerged in 2023 offering high six-figure salaries (MIT Technology Review 2024), while AI Ethicist, AI Product Manager, and Machine Learning Operations Specialist roles are now mainstream, with projected growth of 29–45% through the late 2020s (Atera 2026). National University (2026) reported that 14% of workers already report AI-related displacement, while entry-level software engineering job postings grew 47% between October 2023 and November 2024 (National University 2026), illustrating AI’s dual nature as both disruptor and creator.

title: AI Careers and Industry Trends:
section: 2 Business Analytics, Data Science, and Machine Learning Trends
text:
2 Business Analytics, Data Science, and Machine Learning Trends


2.1 Introduction
Business analytics, data science, and machine learning have become among the most consequential professional fields of the twenty-first century. As organizations across every sector compete to extract value from growing volumes of data, demand for skilled analysts and data scientists has surged (IEEE-USA InSight 2026). The integration of generative AI has further accelerated this transformation — raising the technical bar for practitioners while simultaneously automating routine analytical tasks (Gartner 2024). This section examines the most in-demand skills, how job requirements evolved in 2024, which industries are hiring most aggressively, and what the career outlook holds for professionals in these fields.


2.2 Research Rationale
This analysis leverages the Lightcast 2024 job postings dataset (Lightcast 2024) to investigate current employer demand for business analytics, data science, and machine learning professionals. Rather than relying solely on projected employment figures, this study examines real job postings to identify which technical skills — such as Python, SQL, NLP, and cloud platforms — appear most frequently, which industries are actively hiring data professionals, and how compensation varies by role, location, and skill requirements. This approach provides a ground-level view of the 2024 job market that complements broader labor market forecasts from sources such as the BLS (U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics 2025a) and WEF (World Economic Forum 2023).


2.3 Literature Review
The BLS Employment Projections 2024–2034 provide the clearest quantitative baseline. Data scientist employment is projected to grow from 245,900 to 328,300 positions — a 33.5% increase driven by AI model development and enterprise application integration (U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics 2025a). Operations research analysts are projected at 21% growth, management analysts at 9%, and market research analysts at 7% (U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics 2025d, 2025b, 2025c).
The AnalytixLabs Data Science Skills Survey (2024), drawing on 100+ Fortune 500 hiring managers, found data engineering was the most-recruited function (76.3%), followed by data scientist (73.7%) and machine learning engineer (57.9%) roles (AnalytixLabs 2024). A 365 Data Science analysis of 1,000 job postings found that NLP skill demand surged from 5% in 2023 to 19% in 2024, and machine learning was mentioned in over 69% of postings (365 Data Science 2024).
The WEF Future of Jobs Report 2023 projected demand for AI and machine learning specialists to increase 40% by 2027 (World Economic Forum 2023). Gartner projected that over 50% of data science tasks would be automated by 2025 through AutoML tools (Gartner 2024), but research consistently finds these tools augment rather than replace skilled professionals. Coursera (2025) noted that business analyst demand is projected to generate approximately 98,100 job openings annually through 2034, with median wages ranging from $78,400 to over $101,000 (Coursera 2025).

title: Data Cleaning & Preprocessing
text:
3. Data Cleaning


Starting shape: (72498, 131)



3.1 Drop Tracking & Redundant Columns
We remove metadata columns (hash IDs, URLs, internal tracking) and older NAICS / SOC hierarchy levels that are superseded by the 2022 / 2021 versions we keep.
Columns retained:

NAICS_2022_2 through NAICS_2022_6 (and their _NAME variants) — current industry classification
SOC_2021_2 through SOC_2021_5 (and _NAME variants) — current occupation classification
All LOT_* occupation taxonomy columns



Dropped 18 columns: ['ID', 'URL', 'ACTIVE_URLS', 'ACTIVE_SOURCES_INFO', 'DUPLICATES', 'LAST_UPDATED_TIMESTAMP', 'LAST_UPDATED_DATE', 'NAICS2', 'NAICS3', 'NAICS4', 'NAICS5', 'NAICS6', 'SOC_2', 'SOC_2_NAME', 'SOC_3', 'SOC_3_NAME', 'SOC_5', 'SOC_5_NAME']
Shape after step 3.1: (72498, 113)




3.2 Drop High-Missing Columns (with Salary Protection)
We drop columns where more than 50 % of values are missing, but explicitly protect salary and education columns from this rule.
Why protect salary columns? SALARY, SALARY_FROM, and SALARY_TO are each ~55–58 % missing because many employers choose not to post salary information — this is normal for job postings data, not a data quality issue. The remaining ~42 % (≈ 30,000 rows) provides a sufficient sample for salary analysis. Dropping them would silently break all downstream salary work.


Protected columns: ['SALARY', 'SALARY_FROM', 'SALARY_TO', 'ORIGINAL_PAY_PERIOD', 'MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE', 'MAX_YEARS_EXPERIENCE', 'LIGHTCAST_SECTORS', 'LIGHTCAST_SECTORS_NAME', 'MIN_EDULEVELS', 'MIN_EDULEVELS_NAME', 'MAX_EDULEVELS', 'MAX_EDULEVELS_NAME']
Dropped 0 high-missing non-protected columns.
Shape after step 3.2: (72498, 113)




3.3 Remove Duplicate Postings
We deduplicate on the combination of job title + company + state + posting date. This removes re-posted or syndicated listings that represent the same position.


Deduplication key: ['TITLE_RAW', 'COMPANY_NAME', 'STATE_NAME', 'POSTED']
Removed 4,926 duplicate rows (6.8% of pre-dedup total).
Shape after step 3.3: (67572, 113)




3.4 Build Unified Salary Column (SALARY_MID)
We create a single salary column by taking the midpoint of SALARY_FROM and SALARY_TO. Where the midpoint is unavailable we fall back to the SALARY column.

Important: Missing salary values are left as NaN — we do not impute them with the median or mean. All downstream salary analyses should use .dropna(subset=["SALARY_MID"]) or filter to df[df["SALARY_MID"].notna()] to work only with real data, preserving the true salary distribution.



Created SALARY_MID = (SALARY_FROM + SALARY_TO) / 2  [fallback: SALARY]

Salary coverage:  30,037 postings with data (44.5%)
Plausible range:  $10,617 – $470,000
Median:           $111,800
Mean:             $114,245




3.5 Fill Categorical Missing Values
Remaining NaN values in key categorical columns are replaced with the string "Unknown" so they appear explicitly in charts and model features rather than being silently excluded.


Filled 'Unknown' in:
  NAICS_2022_6_NAME                              (17 values filled)
  NAICS_2022_2_NAME                              (17 values filled)
  SOC_2021_4_NAME                                (17 values filled)
  REMOTE_TYPE_NAME                               (17 values filled)

title: Skill Gap Analysis
text:
This section evaluates the gap between our team’s current technical skill levels and the skills most demanded in the 2024 job market. Building on our Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA), we focus on key skill categories in data science, business analytics, and machine learning roles. The goal is to identify strengths, highlight gaps, and provide actionable recommendations for career development.



Based on our EDA of the Lightcast dataset, job postings in data and analytics roles emphasize the following skill groups:

Programming: Python, R, Scala

Core Data Skills: SQL, statistics, data analysis, data visualization

Traditional BI Tools: Tableau, Power BI, Excel

Cloud Platforms: AWS, Azure, Databricks, Snowflake

AI/ML Tools: TensorFlow, PyTorch, scikit-learn, MLflow, SageMaker

These findings indicate that employers increasingly expect candidates to combine strong analytical foundations with technical and cloud-based capabilities.

The largest gaps appear in cloud platforms, machine learning, and AI-related tools. The team is relatively closer to market expectations in Excel, Python, and basic data analysis skills.
This indicates that while the team has a solid analytical foundation, there is a need to strengthen more advanced technical capabilities to remain competitive in the job market.




To address the identified gaps, the following actions are recommended:

Strengthen Python and SQL proficiency across all team members
Learn cloud platforms such as AWS or Azure
Develop machine learning skills using libraries like scikit-learn
Improve data visualization skills using Tableau, Power BI, or Plotly
Work on real-world projects to integrate analysis, modelling, and visualization




Overall, the team demonstrates a strong foundation in core analytics skills. However, gaps in advanced technical areas such as cloud computing and machine learning suggest the need for continued skill development.
By aligning learning efforts with market demand, the team can significantly improve its competitiveness in business analytics and data science roles.
